bio.title: Rev.
bio.bio_blurb: Yale-educated minister of wandering Connecticut pulpits

narrative_blocks.header: A Yale minister with a wandering pulpit
narrative_blocks.body: Ebenezer Martin graduated from Yale College in 1756 and was ordained in 1759 as the first pastor of the church at Becket, Massachusetts. His career proved restless: he was dismissed in 1764 amid disputes over land ownership and unspecified personal indiscretions.

narrative_blocks.header: Debt chased him from parish to parish
narrative_blocks.body: After Becket he preached at Groton, then Ashford, where by 1772 his debts grew so deep he petitioned the Colony assembly to escape imprisonment. He continued moving through New York and Pennsylvania pulpits for the rest of his life.

narrative_blocks.header: He sent his son to a famous grandfather
narrative_blocks.body: Ebenezer and Susanna Plumb married around 1758 and had several children. Their son Joseph, raised partly by affluent Milford grandparents, would become one of the most quoted common soldiers of the Revolution.

bio.title: Rev.
bio.bio_blurb: Yale-educated minister of wandering Connecticut pulpits

narrative_blocks.body: After Becket he preached at Groton, then Ashford, where by 1772 his debts grew so deep he petitioned the Colony assembly to escape imprisonment. He continued moving through New York and Pennsylvania pulpits for the rest of his life.

narrative_blocks.body: Ebenezer and Susanna Plumb married around 1758 and had several children. Their son Joseph, raised partly by affluent Milford grandparents, would become one of the most quoted common soldiers of the Revolution.

notable.notable_blurb: Revolutionary War memoirist, Sappers and Miners sergeant

narrative_blocks.header: A teenager who vowed to run to war
narrative_blocks.body: Fifteen and eager after Lexington and Concord, Joseph Plumb Martin won his grandparents’ consent to enlist only by threatening to run off as a privateer. He joined the Connecticut Militia in June 1776 and reached New York just as the British Long Island campaign opened.

narrative_blocks.header: He cleared the way for Hamilton at Yorktown
narrative_blocks.body: Promoted to sergeant in Washington’s new Corps of Sappers and Miners, Martin dug the entrenchments before Yorktown. During the siege his corps cleared the abatis so Alexander Hamilton’s regiment could storm Redoubt #10.

narrative_blocks.header: His memoir became history’s common soldier
narrative_blocks.body: Published anonymously in 1830 and lost for over a century, his narrative resurfaced in the 1950s to become one of the finest primary accounts of the Revolution. Historians prize it precisely because Martin was no general but an ordinary private.

narrative_blocks.header: A war hero who died owning nothing
narrative_blocks.body: Settling Maine’s frontier, Martin lost a bitter land dispute to Henry Knox and was insolvent by 1818, when a $96 annual pension finally came. He served his town of Prospect as clerk for twenty-five years.

narrative_blocks.header: Hollywood and a marching salute remembered him
narrative_blocks.body: In 1836 a passing platoon of Light Infantry fired a salute outside his home. He has since been portrayed by Philip Seymour Hoffman among others, and a trail around Valley Forge bears his name.

military_service.unit: 8th Connecticut Regiment; Corps of Sappers and Miners
military_service.notes: Enlisted Connecticut Militia 1776 at age 15; Continental Army 1777. Corporal in Light Infantry 1778; Sergeant in Sappers & Miners 1780. Present at Yorktown clearing abatis ahead of Hamilton’s assault on Redoubt #10.

cross_connections.display_label: shared a Plumb grandmother, unknown to either soldier
